Schwarcz & Wolff — The Limits of Regulating AI Safety Through Liability and Insurance: Lessons from Cybersecurity

Schwarcz and Wolff argue that proposals to regulate AI safety through tort liability and insurance are unlikely to succeed, drawing on two decades of failed efforts to regulate cybersecurity through similar mechanisms. Insurers have struggled to price cyber risk accurately or incentivize meaningful security improvements, and the authors contend that AI safety risks — characterized by opacity, rapid technological change, and lack of standardized loss data — pose even greater challenges. Rather than relying primarily on liability and insurance, the Article advocates for comprehensive ex ante regulation, beginning with mandatory, standardized collection and disclosure of AI safety data.
